narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile—including your undergraduate GPA of 2.40 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 25 (well under the average 37), and school decile rankings of 2nd in both Year 1 and Year 3 (compared to averages around 5th or 6th)—and calculated a 48% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass with high confidence. Among all the factors in your profile, three stood out as having the strongest influence on this decision. Your middle-income family background was the most impactful positive factor, nudging the prediction toward a higher chance of passing the bar, likely because students from similar circumstances have historically shown solid performance. However, your lower Year 3 decile ranking played a significant counteracting role, pushing the model strongly toward predicting failure, as it signals academic performance lagging behind most peers at that stage. Similarly, your LSAT score had a notable negative pull, reinforcing the failure risk due to how far it trails the scores of students who typically succeed. Overall, while your family income provided some uplift, the combination of your Year 3 standing and LSAT score tipped the scales against you compared to typical admitted students, who often have stronger metrics in these areas. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your academic decile through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience from a middle-income background. Reapplying after addressing these key areas could significantly improve your predicted success odds and open doors at other programs.
